refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is available for productive operation to total scheduled operational time, excluding planned maintenance windows.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refining it requires explicit definition of: (1) the measurement period and baseline, (2) what constitutes 'available' vs. 'unavailable' (e.g., excludes planned maintenance, includes unplanned downtime), (3) the scope of measurement (entire NGHE solution or subsystems), and (4) acceptance criteria alignment with customer operational policies. High safety criticality demands that availability targets not compromise safety—i.e., the system must remain safe even during degraded availability states.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous telemetry logging of system state transitions (operational, degraded, unavailable, maintenance) across all subsystems; statistical analysis of uptime vs. downtime over rolling evaluation windows; correlation with incident reports and maintenance records to distinguish planned vs. unplanned downtime.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall detect and log all state transitions (operational, degraded, unavailable) in real-time.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall minimize unplanned downtime through predictive maintenance and fault tolerance.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall provide availability metrics and reporting to stakeholders at agreed intervals.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall maintain safe operation during degraded availability states (e.g., loss of teleoperation, sensor failures).

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) with automatic failover and health monitoring to ensure continuous operation despite component failures.
DP-B: Implement predictive maintenance scheduling and spare parts inventory management to minimize unplanned downtime and extend mean time between failures (MTBF).
DP-C: Implement real-time availability monitoring and reporting dashboard that tracks system state, logs transitions, and alerts stakeholders to degradation.

DP-D: Implement graceful degradation modes that allow safe operation with reduced capability (e.g., manual override, reduced speed) when subsystems fail, maintaining availability while preserving safety.
selected_dp.selected_dp: Integrated approach combining DP-A (redundancy and failover), DP-C (monitoring and reporting), and DP-D (graceful degradation). This multi-faceted design parameter addresses availability through three complementary mechanisms: (1) preventing failures via redundancy, (2) detecting and responding to failures via monitoring, and (3) maintaining safe operation during failures via graceful degradation. DP-B (predictive maintenance) is deferred to lower decomposition levels as it is a supporting operational process rather than a system design parameter.

potential_sources: Redundancy (DP-A) and graceful degradation (DP-D) are tightly coupled: redundant subsystems must be designed to fail independently, and degradation modes must be coordinated across subsystems to avoid cascading failures.
Monitoring (DP-C) must be decoupled from the subsystems it monitors to avoid single points of failure; monitoring infrastructure itself must be redundant.
Safety criticality (high) couples with all three DPs: redundancy, monitoring, and degradation must all preserve safety invariants.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP is not fully decoupled but represents a necessary integration of three design principles. Coupling is manageable through clear interface definitions (Telemetry, SafetyAlert) and explicit failure mode analysis. Recommend detailed FMEA at next decomposition level.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Redundant subsystems can be designed to fail independently (no common-mode failures). Risk: If Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services share common infrastructure (e.g., power, network), redundancy may be illusory.

Assumption: Real-time monitoring can detect all failure modes with sufficient latency to trigger failover. Risk: Silent failures (e.g., sensor drift, software bugs) may not be detected, reducing effective availability.

Assumption: Graceful degradation modes are acceptable to the operator and do not violate safety constraints. Risk: Operator may not accept reduced capability; safety analysis may prohibit certain degradation modes.

Assumption: Customer-defined availability target (≥0.97) is achievable with current technology and cost constraints. Risk: Achieving 97% availability may require expensive redundancy or operational changes that exceed project budget.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP to explicitly separate concerns: (1) DP-A.1: Redundancy strategy (which subsystems are redundant, failover logic), (2) DP-C.1: Monitoring strategy (what is monitored, detection latency, alert thresholds), (3) DP-D.1: Degradation strategy (which modes are safe, operator training requirements). This separation will enable clearer allocation to implementation modules and reduce coupling.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution is the top-level system boundary that integrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. It is responsible for orchestrating redundancy, failover, monitoring, and graceful degradation across all subsystems to achieve the availability KPI. The NGHE Solution module owns the availability architecture, including redundancy logic, health monitoring, and state management.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution emits real-time telemetry data (system state, subsystem health, availability metrics) to external monitoring and reporting systems. This interface carries availability-critical information.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution emits safety alerts when availability degradation impacts safety (e.g., loss of teleoperation capability, sensor failures). This interface is critical for safety-aware failover and operator notification.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives operational tasking from Site Manager or Tele-operator. During degraded availability, tasking may be constrained (e.g., reduced speed, manual-only mode). This interface must support availability-aware task negotiation.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution depends on Network Link for communication between Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. Network Link redundancy is critical for availability; loss of Network Link triggers graceful degradation.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of the availability KPI and the orchestration logic for redundancy, failover, and monitoring. Availability is a system-level property that emerges from the integration of subsystems, so it must be owned at the top level. Minor diffusion risk: some availability logic (e.g., local health checks) may be distributed to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, but the overall availability architecture and failover orchestration must be centralized in NGHE Solution.

Vehicle is responsible for its own operational availability (e.g., propulsion, steering, sensors), but cannot own the system-level availability KPI. If availability logic is distributed to Vehicle, it will be fragmented and difficult to reason about. Vehicle should implement local health monitoring and report status via Telemetry, but failover orchestration must be in NGHE Solution.

Teleoperation is responsible for its own availability (e.g., network connectivity, operator interface), but cannot own the system-level availability KPI. Similar to Vehicle, Teleoperation should report health status but not orchestrate system-level failover.

Safety Services is responsible for safety-critical availability (e.g., ensuring safe operation during degradation), but the overall availability KPI is broader than safety. Safety Services should implement safety-aware failover logic and emit SafetyAlert, but the availability orchestration must be in NGHE Solution.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the appropriate owner of the availability KPI because: (1) availability is a system-level emergent property that depends on the integration of Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, (2) the selected DP (redundancy, monitoring, graceful degradation) requires orchestration across subsystems, which must be centralized to avoid diffusion and coupling, (3) the external interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, Network Link) are all system-level concerns that NGHE Solution must manage. Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) should implement local health monitoring and report status, but not own the system-level availability architecture.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution may become a 'god module' that owns too many concerns (availability, safety, tasking, networking). Mitigation: Use clear interface contracts (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, Network Link) to decouple NGHE Solution from subsystem internals. NGHE Solution should orchestrate via interfaces, not direct subsystem control.

Risk: Redundancy and failover logic in NGHE Solution may be complex and difficult to verify. Mitigation: Decompose availability logic into sub-modules (e.g., Redundancy Manager, Health Monitor, Degradation Manager) at the next level.

Risk: Network Link is a critical dependency for NGHE Solution's availability orchestration. If Network Link fails, NGHE Solution cannot coordinate failover. Mitigation: Implement local failover logic in Vehicle and Safety Services that operates independently of Network Link; NGHE Solution coordinates when Network Link is available.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine IM assignment to explicitly define the boundary between NGHE Solution and subsystems: (1) NGHE Solution owns system-level availability orchestration (redundancy strategy, failover logic, state machine), (2) Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services own local health monitoring and report status via Telemetry, (3) Safety Services owns safety-aware failover logic and emits SafetyAlert, (4) NGHE Solution coordinates failover based on Telemetry and SafetyAlert. This separation of concerns will reduce diffusion and clarify ownership.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The selected DP (redundancy, monitoring, graceful degradation) directly addresses the FR (achieve ≥0.97 availability). The DP is logically sound: redundancy prevents single points of failure, monitoring detects failures, and graceful degradation maintains safe operation during failures. The DP is not over-constrained or under-specified; it provides sufficient guidance for the next decomposition level without prescribing implementation details. Coupling is manageable through interface definitions.

diffusion_risks: Moderate diffusion risk: Redundancy logic may be distributed across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services if not carefully managed. Mitigation: NGHE Solution must own the redundancy strategy and failover orchestration; subsystems implement local health checks only.

Moderate diffusion risk: Monitoring logic may be scattered across multiple modules if each subsystem implements its own health monitoring. Mitigation: Define a standard Telemetry interface that all subsystems use to report health; NGHE Solution aggregates and analyzes telemetry.
Low diffusion risk: Graceful degradation logic is primarily in NGHE Solution (state machine, mode transitions) with support from Safety Services (safety validation). This is well-contained.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate owner of the availability DP. The module boundary is clear: NGHE Solution orchestrates availability across subsystems via well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, Network Link). Subsystems are responsible for local health monitoring and reporting, not for system-level availability decisions. This containment strategy minimizes diffusion and clarifies ownership.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of the availability KPI is feasible through continuous telemetry logging and statistical analysis. The NGHE Solution module must emit detailed Telemetry data (system state, subsystem health, failure events) that can be analyzed to compute availability metrics. Verification requires: (1) clear definition of 'available' vs. 'unavailable' states, (2) automated logging of state transitions, (3) statistical analysis tools to compute availability over evaluation periods. These are all within the scope of NGHE Solution.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The allocation of the availability KPI to NGHE Solution is clear. The module owns the availability architecture and is responsible for achieving the target. Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) are responsible for their own operational reliability, which contributes to system availability. The allocation is hierarchical and non-overlapping: NGHE Solution owns the system-level KPI, subsystems own component-level reliability.

recommended_revisions: Refine the FR to explicitly define the measurement period, baseline, and acceptance criteria. Coordinate with the customer to ensure the ≥0.97 target is achievable and aligned with operational policies.

Refine the DP to separate concerns: (1) redundancy strategy (which subsystems are redundant, failover logic), (2) monitoring strategy (what is monitored, detection latency), (3) degradation strategy (which modes are safe). This will enable clearer allocation to sub-modules at the next level.

Refine the IM assignment to define the boundary between NGHE Solution and subsystems more explicitly. Specify the Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, and Network Link interfaces in detail to ensure subsystems can report health and NGHE Solution can orchestrate failover.

Conduct a preliminary FMEA to identify failure modes that could impact availability (e.g., Network Link failure, sensor failure, software bug) and verify that the selected DP (redundancy, monitoring, graceful degradation) adequately mitigates them.

decision.justification: The refined FR, selected DP, and assigned IM are logically sound and pass all gates. However, the DP is not fully atomic and requires further decomposition to clarify the redundancy strategy, monitoring strategy, and degradation strategy. The IM assignment is clear (NGHE Solution owns the availability KPI), but the internal architecture of NGHE Solution (how it orchestrates redundancy, monitoring, and degradation) must be decomposed at the next level. Proceeding to the next decomposition level will enable more detailed design and verification planning.
decision.justification_detail: CR-NGHE-004 is a KPI that emerges from the integration of multiple subsystems. The selected DP (redundancy, monitoring, graceful degradation) is a high-level strategy that must be decomposed into more specific design parameters for each sub-FR. The sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-004.1 through FR-NGHE-004.4) are well-defined and ready for the next decomposition level. NGHE Solution is the appropriate owner, but its internal architecture (how it implements redundancy, monitoring, and degradation) must be designed in detail.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall detect and log all state transitions (operational, degraded, unavailable) in real-time.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall minimize unplanned downtime through predictive maintenance and fault tolerance.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall provide availability metrics and reporting to stakeholders at agreed intervals.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall maintain safe operation during degraded availability states (e.g., loss of teleoperation, sensor failures).

assumptions: Assumption: Redundant subsystems can be designed to fail independently (no common-mode failures).
Assumption: Real-time monitoring can detect all failure modes with sufficient latency to trigger failover.
Assumption: Graceful degradation modes are acceptable to the operator and do not violate safety constraints.
Assumption: Customer-defined availability target (≥0.97) is achievable with current technology and cost constraints.
Assumption: NGHE Solution can orchestrate failover across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services via well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, Network Link).
Assumption: Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) will implement local health monitoring and report status via Telemetry interface.
open_risks: Risk: Common-mode failures in redundant subsystems could reduce effective availability below target.
Risk: Silent failures (e.g., sensor drift, software bugs) may not be detected by monitoring, reducing effective availability.
Risk: Operator may not accept graceful degradation modes (e.g., reduced speed, manual-only operation), limiting availability in practice.
Risk: Achieving 97% availability may require expensive redundancy or operational changes that exceed project budget.
Risk: Network Link failure could prevent NGHE Solution from coordinating failover across subsystems.
Risk: Safety criticality (high) may constrain availability design (e.g., prohibiting certain degradation modes), making the target difficult to achieve.
evidence_needed: Customer requirements document specifying the availability target (≥0.97), measurement period, and acceptance criteria.
Preliminary FMEA identifying failure modes that could impact availability and their mitigation strategies.
Redundancy analysis showing that subsystems can fail independently and that failover logic is correct.
Monitoring strategy document specifying what is monitored, detection latency, and alert thresholds.
Graceful degradation strategy document specifying which modes are safe and operator training requirements.
Cost-benefit analysis showing that the selected DP (redundancy, monitoring, graceful degradation) is achievable within project constraints.
Safety analysis (e.g., HARA, FMEA) showing that availability design does not compromise safety.
